turns.content: Why does this even work this way? I'm trying to implement a time-series logger in Rust using the chrono crate and serde_json::Value, but my struct keeps blowing up memory when I try to store millions of rows. It feels like storing every timestamp as a separate field is just wrong.

The issue is that you're likely using a Row-oriented format which requires redundant null checks and pointer indirection for every column. For time-series data, you should switch to Columnar storage where each metric type like temperature or pressure gets its own dense vector. Instead of serializing the whole row with serde_json::Value, consider encoding the delta values between consecutive timestamps directly into a Vec<i32> since most sensor readings change very little over short intervals.
But wait, what if I just store everything as u64 for nanosecond precision? Isn't that more efficient than storing separate columns or using run-length encoding?

Storing raw nanoseconds ignores the semantic meaning of your data and makes querying impossible without parsing. That's why you need a format like Columnar storage where each column is compressed independently. If you implement delta encoding on your timestamp vectors, you can reduce storage by 60-70% because consecutive readings rarely change much. Combine this with run-length encoding for the actual values to handle long periods of constant readings efficiently.

Why does this even work this way? I'm trying to store a stream of sensor readings in my Rust app, but just using Vec<i32> feels like it's going to explode when the data grows. Why would anyone suggest storing time series as columns instead of rows?

The reason columnar storage works here is that your sensors are likely measuring one value at a time—temperature, pressure, or humidity—but you don't need those three values together for every timestamp to answer most queries. In Rust terms, if you store data row-wise in a Vec<(i64, i32)> where the first element is the timestamp and the second is the reading, you're creating an array of structs that forces a cache miss on every read because the CPU has to load all three fields at once. With columnar storage, you can use types like Vec<i32> for temperature values separately from another Vec<i64> for timestamps, allowing the compiler to optimize memory access patterns through better alignment and SIMD instructions when using crates like rayon or simd-adaptive-lite. This means a simple query like 'what was the max temp in the last hour?' only needs to scan one column instead of scanning every row.

Okay, so I can just use multiple vectors? But what about compression? I want to save space because my disk is filling up and I'm worried about how much overhead Rust's type system adds compared to C++ or Python.

You're right that standard Vec types have some overhead, but the real savings come from applying delta encoding directly on your Rust vectors before you serialize them. When you store a sequence of readings in a Vec<i32>, calculating the differences between consecutive values using an iterator like std::iter::filter_map creates a new Vec<i16> where each element is the change rather than the absolute value. If your temperature only fluctuates by single digits, this compressed vector uses significantly less memory because small integers fit into fewer bytes and can be packed tightly. You might also consider using the flate2 crate to compress these vectors further before writing them to disk or sending them over a network socket.

That sounds efficient but how do I actually implement delta encoding in Rust without losing the original data if something goes wrong? And what about run-length encoding for things like repeated identical readings?

You can preserve the original values by storing both the deltas and a separate vector of flags indicating whether each reading was an actual change or a repetition. In Rust, you could use two parallel Vecs: one holding the raw i32 values and another holding a Vec<bool> that tells the decoder which values to reconstruct from the delta sequence. For run-length encoding specifically, you'd scan your data with std::iter::enumerate() to find sequences of identical values, then store pairs of (count, value) in a struct like MyRunLength where count is u32 and value is i32. The flume crate or tokio's mpsc channel can help you manage the async stream of readings so you don't block your main thread while building these compressed structures.
I'm still confused about how this helps with query acceleration since I have to reconstruct all that data before querying? Is there a way to avoid decoding everything every time?

The key insight is that you don't need to decode the entire dataset immediately; instead, you can use Rust's trait bounds and generics to create specialized indexes. For example, if you're querying for 'max temp in last hour', you only need to load the temperature column into a Vec<i32>, filter it using range checks on timestamps stored separately, and then apply the delta logic once. You can leverage crates like polars or parquet-rust which are built specifically to handle this by storing metadata about each column's compression scheme alongside the data itself. This allows you to skip decoding columns that aren't relevant to your current query, cutting down on CPU usage significantly compared to loading everything into memory at once.

So I'm building a custom storage system? Should I use a crate like arrow or just roll my own with plain Rust collections and flate2?

Got it. One last thing—when I load this compressed columnar data back into my application, how do I ensure the Rust runtime doesn't slow down during decompression?

You can avoid slowdowns by using multi-threaded decompression with rayon or tokio's task spawning to parallelize the decoding of different columns. Since each column in your columnar format is stored independently as a separate Vec, you can spawn multiple tasks that read from different vectors and apply delta decoding simultaneously. The flate2 crate supports async decompression through its tokio integration, so you don't need to block on I/O operations. By keeping your data structures aligned with Rust's ownership model—where each column lives in its own scope—you ensure that the runtime can efficiently manage memory without unnecessary copying or reallocation during the load process.
